meta.subject: physics
meta.topic_name: Stopping of Block Due to Friction

intro.kicker: Topic 17 of 22 • Chapter: Friction • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.topicName: Stopping of Block Due to Friction
intro.description: A block moving with initial velocity u on a rough surface decelerates due to kinetic friction and comes to rest. On a horizontal surface: deceleration a = μg; stopping distance S = u²/(2μg); stopping time t = u/(μg). On a rough incline at angle θ: deceleration a = g(sinθ + μcosθ); stopping distance S = u²/[2g(sinθ + μcosθ)]. Key NEET insight: S is independent of mass (m cancels). The textbook also expresses S in terms of momentum: S = P²/(2μm²g), linking kinematics with momentum.

expectedQuestions.description: High-frequency NEET numerical topic. Tests: (i) find S given u and μ on horizontal surface; (ii) compare stopping distances for two blocks of equal velocity but different masses (same μ) — answer: equal; (iii) stopping distance on incline given u, μ, θ. Appears in most NEET papers as a direct substitution or conceptual mass-independence question.

timeRequired.description: 30 min for horizontal and incline derivations from Newton’s second law + kinematics, 30 min on past NEET MCQs. The key mastery task: derive S = u²/(2μg) from v² = u² − 2aS with a = μg. Memorise both horizontal and incline forms. Special attention to the incline formula: deceleration includes both sinθ (component of gravity) AND μcosθ (friction term).

difficulty.description: Concept is simple but NEET traps lie in the incline formula. On a horizontal surface, a = μg (pure friction). On an incline, a = g(sinθ + μcosθ) — gravity decelerates the block along the slope AND friction decelerates it. Students frequently forget the sinθ term and write only μcosθ. Also: mass cancels from S = u²/(2μg), so two blocks of different masses but same u and μ stop in the same distance.

nriUsaGap.description: AP Physics covers stopping distance on horizontal surfaces. However, NEET extends this to inclined planes with friction and requires the combined deceleration a = g(sinθ + μcosθ). The NEET-specific depth: expressing S in terms of initial momentum P = mu as S = P²/(2μm²g). US students will recognise the horizontal formula but may miss the momentum form and the incline generalisation.

weightage.sectionTitle: Stopping of Block Due to Friction — NEET Weightage Analysis (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: Consistent 1 question per year in NEET 2019–2024.
Mostly numerical: find S or t given u and μ.
Conceptual variant: does S depend on mass? (No.)

strategySteps.title: Derive from Newton’s Second Law
strategySteps.description: F_friction = μmg; ma = μmg; a = μg. Apply v² = u² − 2aS with v = 0: S = u²/(2μg). Apply v = u − at with v = 0: t = u/(μg). Both derivations take 30 seconds once the acceleration is known.

strategySteps.title: Extend to Inclined Surface
strategySteps.description: On incline at angle θ, retardation is a = g(sinθ + μcosθ). Substituting into v² = u² − 2aS: S = u²/[2g(sinθ + μcosθ)]. Both gravity component (along incline) and friction act to decelerate the block moving upward. This is the NEET-critical extension.

strategySteps.title: Memorise Mass-Independence
strategySteps.description: S = u²/(2μg) contains no mass term. A 5 kg block and a 50 kg block with the same u and same μ stop in identical distances. NEET sets questions where students incorrectly expect heavier blocks to stop farther (because larger momentum) — they do not, because friction is also larger in proportion.

cards.title: Stopping Distance Complete Notes
cards.description: Full derivations for horizontal and inclined surfaces, momentum form S = P²/(2μm²g), comparison table for different u and μ, and 8 solved NEET-pattern MCQs.

cards.title: Incline Stopping Formula Card
cards.description: Quick-reference card: horizontal formula, incline formula, momentum form, and the mass-independence conceptual proof. One page for last-night revision.

subtopicsIndex.sectionTitle: Subtopics in Stopping of Block Due to Friction

cards.title: Horizontal Surface Stopping
cards.coreRevision: Retarding force F = μmg; acceleration a = μg. From v² = u² − 2aS with v = 0: S = u²/(2μg). Also expressed as S = P²/(2μm²g) where P = mu. Stopping time t = u/(μg). Both S and t are independent of mass.
points: a = μg (retardation on horizontal rough surface)
If u doubles, S quadruples (S ∝ u²)

cards.title: Inclined Surface Stopping
cards.coreRevision: Block moves up a rough incline at angle θ with initial velocity u. Both gravity component (mg sinθ) and friction (mg μ cosθ) oppose motion. Total retardation a = g(sinθ + μcosθ). Stopping distance: S = u²/[2g(sinθ + μcosθ)].
points: Retardation on incline: a = g(sinθ + μcosθ)
Both sinθ and μcosθ decelerate the block
If θ = 0: reduces to S = u²/(2μg) — a good self-check
S given by the denominator sum, not difference

curriculum.hintText: How AP Physics compares to NEET on stopping-distance analysis.

gaps.title: Incline Stopping Not Standard in AP
gaps.description: AP Physics teaches stopping distance on horizontal surfaces but the incline formula with combined sinθ and μcosθ deceleration is not standard. NEET explicitly tests this combined form and expects immediate recall of S = u²/[2g(sinθ + μcosθ)] under examination conditions.
bullets: The ‘+’ sign in the denominator: both friction AND gravity oppose upward sliding.
For downward sliding on incline: deceleration = g(μcosθ − sinθ) if μ > tanθ.

gaps.title: Momentum Form of Stopping Distance
gaps.description: S = P²/(2μm²g) connects the stopping distance to initial momentum. This form appears in NEET problems that give P instead of u. US courses always work with velocity, not momentum, in stopping-distance problems.
bullets: Substitute P = mu into S = u²/(2μg) to get S = P²/(2μm²g).
If two blocks have equal momentum P but different masses, the lighter one stops in a greater distance (S ∝ 1/m² for fixed P).

questions.question: A body moving with speed 12 m/s on a rough horizontal surface (μ = 0.4) stops after travelling:
A block of 5 kg and another of 10 kg, both moving at 15 m/s on the same rough horizontal surface (μ = 0.25), will stop after:

options.text: Both stop at the same distance 45 m
They stop at distances proportional to their masses

questions.explanation: S = u²/(2μg) = 225/(2×0.25×10) = 225/5 = 45 m. Mass does not appear in the formula; both blocks stop at 45 m. This is the classic mass-independence NEET trap.

questions.question: If initial velocity is doubled, the stopping distance on a horizontal rough surface:
A block slides up a rough incline (30°, μ = 0.5). The retardation (g = 10 m/s²) is approximately:
For a block with momentum P on a horizontal rough surface, stopping distance is:
Stopping distance does NOT depend on:

items.title: Memorise both stopping formulas
items.description: Horizontal: S = u²/(2μg). Incline (moving up rough slope): S = u²/[2g(sinθ + μcosθ)]. Verify by setting θ = 0 in the second to recover the first.

items.title: Confirm mass-independence
items.description: Write S = u²/(2μg). There is no m in this expression. If NEET gives two blocks with the same initial velocity on the same surface, they stop at the same distance regardless of their masses.

items.title: Derive stopping time formula
items.description: t = u/(μg) on horizontal surface. Derived from v = u − at with v = 0 and a = μg. Time also does not depend on mass.

items.question: Why doesn’t stopping distance depend on mass?
items.answer: The friction force F_k = μmg is proportional to mass. The acceleration a = F_k/m = μg. The mass cancels, making a mass-independent. Since S = u²/(2a) = u²/(2μg), it also has no mass dependence. This contrasts with momentum: greater mass implies greater momentum at same u, but also proportionally greater friction to slow it down.

items.question: On an incline, why does the formula have sinθ + μcosθ in the denominator?
items.answer: When a block moves up a rough incline, both gravity (component along incline = mg sinθ) and kinetic friction (f = μmg cosθ) oppose the motion. Total retarding force = mg(sinθ + μcosθ). Net deceleration a = g(sinθ + μcosθ), leading to S = u²/[2g(sinθ + μcosθ)].

items.question: What if the block slides down a rough incline? Is the formula different?
items.answer: Yes. When sliding down, gravity accelerates the block (component mg sinθ) while friction decelerates it (f = μmg cosθ, opposing downward motion). Net acceleration = g(sinθ − μcosθ) if sinθ > μcosθ (i.e., angle > angle of repose). The stopping distance while moving upward should not be confused with the sliding-down case.

grounding_summary: Page 243 derives stopping distance on a horizontal surface: retarding force F = μmg gives a = μg; from v² = u² − 2aS and v = 0, S = u²/(2μg). The momentum form S = P²/(2μm²g) is also given. Stopping time t = u/(μg) is derived from v = u − at.

Page 244 extends the formula to inclines: when block starts with velocity u on a rough incline, the combined deceleration a = g(sinθ + μcosθ) gives stopping distance S = u²/[2g(sinθ + μcosθ)]. The textbook labels this as case (2) on inclined road.
grounding_excerpt: Distance travelled before coming to rest: A block of mass
Time taken to come to rest

sources.title: NCERT Physics Class 11 Part 1 — Chapter 5: Laws of Motion (Friction)
sources.why_used: Primary NCERT source establishing kinetic friction deceleration and kinematics used to derive stopping distance formulas.

sources.title: NEET Physics Friction MCQs Set A — StudiesToday
sources.why_used: Verified NEET-level MCQ bank confirming stopping distance as the most frequently tested friction numerical in NEET papers.

review.notes: Full replacement of malformed prior JSON. S = u^2/(2mu*g), t = u/(mu*g) for horizontal; S = u^2/[2g(sin+mu*cos)] for incline. Mass-independence confirmed. Both pages grounded.
